does: This paper introduces IndicGuard, a multilingual safety guardrail system specifically designed for Indic languages. The authors construct a culturally nuanced safety dataset covering ten major Indic languages, systematically capturing region-specific harms, socio-political sensitivities, and adversarial jailbreak attempts. They fine-tune a 4-billion parameter instruction-tuned model based on Gemma-3-4B-IT to function as a real-time content moderator and policy compliance checker. The approach moves beyond English-centric safety mechanisms by incorporating localized categories of harm and regional normative values, delivering a guardrail system that demonstrably improves LLM robustness against culturally-specific vulnerabilities while maintaining practical deployment efficiency.
connects: This work extends the growing body of research on LLM safety and alignment beyond English, building on prior efforts in multilingual NLP and value alignment. It responds to documented limitations in existing safety frameworks that rely heavily on English-language harm taxonomies and fail to capture region-specific risks. The paper connects to earlier work on content moderation at scale, instruction-tuned model fine-tuning, and culturally-aware NLP systems. It also engages with broader concerns about equitable AI deployment across linguistic communities, drawing on emerging recognition that safety mechanisms must be localized rather than universally applied, and building on foundational guardrail architectures adapted here for the Indic context.
enables: IndicGuard establishes infrastructure for safer multilingual LLM deployment across South Asia, enabling future development of region-aware safety standards and benchmarks. The publicly released dataset and model could support downstream research in culturally-grounded content moderation, socio-cultural alignment of LLMs, and harm taxonomy development for underrepresented languages. This work opens pathways for extending similar approaches to other linguistic regions and low-resource language communities, potentially catalyzing broader methodologies for localizing AI safety. It also enables practitioners to implement compliant LLM systems in Indic languages and supports future work in understanding how regional values shape safety requirements, creating foundations for equitable and culturally-appropriate AI governance globally.
